title: Tacoma Narrows Bridge: Galloping Gertie
blurb: Washington, 1940: a beautiful bridge decides the wind is its dance partner, then immediately regrets the choreography.
signatureLine: Never let a bridge freestyle without adult supervision.

cards.text: Budget and design choices produce an unusually slender bridge deck with shallow plate girders instead of a deep stiff truss.
cards.why: The bridge looked sleek, but the deck had too little torsional stiffness for the winds it would face.

cards.text: The long, light span opens in July 1940 and soon earns the nickname 'Galloping Gertie' because it visibly undulates in wind.
cards.why: Early vertical motion showed the bridge and wind were already interacting in unstable ways.

cards.text: On November 7, a steady wind around 40 mph hits the bridge deck.
cards.why: The wind supplied the energy that started the final destructive motion.

cards.text: The deck begins torsional aeroelastic flutter: twisting changes the airflow, and the airflow feeds the twisting.
cards.why: This self-feeding loop, not a simple one-time shove, made the motion grow.

cards.text: The twisting grows so large that parts of the deck reach extreme angles.
cards.why: The flutter transferred wind energy into larger and larger structural motion.

cards.text: Suspenders and deck sections fail, and the center span breaks apart into Puget Sound.
cards.why: The bridge could not survive the repeated torsional stress.

cards.text: Bridge engineers make aerodynamic testing and wind-tunnel studies standard for major spans.
cards.why: The collapse changed the profession's understanding of wind as a design problem.

trap.text: The bridge collapsed because wind matched one exact natural frequency, like a singer breaking a glass.
trap.whyNotCause: That common story confuses correlation with cause. The key mechanism was self-exciting aeroelastic flutter, not simple resonance.
